name: Statistics
description: Introductory statistics covering data collection, descriptive statistics, probability, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and real-world data analysis.

modules.title: Introduction to Data
modules.objectives: Distinguish between categorical and quantitative data; compare observational studies and experiments; identify sampling methods (simple random, stratified, cluster, systematic); recognize sources of bias.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 1.1–1.3: Definitions, Data, Sampling
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Introduction to Planning a Study
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #1 — What Is Statistics?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Identifying Sampling Methods (practice set)
modules.assignment: Find a real news article that references a study. Identify the type of study, sampling method used, and any potential sources of bias. Write a one-page analysis.

modules.title: Displaying Data
modules.objectives: Construct and interpret bar graphs, histograms, box plots, stem-and-leaf plots, and dot plots; choose the most appropriate display for a given data set.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 2.1–2.5: Graphical Displays
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Interpreting Box Plots (practice set)
modules.assignment: Collect a data set of at least 20 values (e.g. daily step counts for a month). Create a histogram, box plot, and dot plot. Describe the shape, center, and spread from each display.

modules.title: Descriptive Statistics: Center & Spread
modules.objectives: Calculate mean, median, and mode; compute range, IQR, variance, and standard deviation; identify outliers using the 1.5×IQR rule.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 2.6–2.8: Measures of Center and Spread
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Standard Deviation
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #3 — Measures of Spread
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Calculating Sample Standard Deviation
modules.assignment: Given a data set of 15 exam scores, compute the mean, median, mode, range, IQR, and standard deviation by hand (show all work). Identify any outliers and explain how removing them changes the measures.

modules.title: Normal Distribution
modules.objectives: Describe the properties of the normal distribution; apply the empirical rule (68-95-99.7); compute z-scores; use the standard normal table to find probabilities and percentiles.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 6.1–6.2: Normal Distributions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Z-Scores and the Empirical Rule
modules.assignment: Heights of students in a school are normally distributed with mean 165 cm and SD 8 cm. Answer 8 questions: compute z-scores, find percentages within ranges using the empirical rule and the z-table, and find the height at a given percentile.

modules.title: Probability Fundamentals
modules.objectives: Define sample space and events; compute probabilities using the addition rule, multiplication rule, and complement rule; distinguish mutually exclusive from independent events.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 3.1–3.4: Probability Basics
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Addition Rule for Probability
modules.assignment: Roll two fair dice. List the complete sample space, then calculate probabilities for 10 events (e.g., sum > 8, at least one 4, both odd). Verify that all complement probabilities are consistent.

modules.title: Conditional Probability & Independence
modules.objectives: Compute conditional probabilities; apply Bayes' theorem; use tree diagrams and contingency tables; test for independence of events.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 3.4–3.6: Conditional Probability & Independence
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Conditional Probability
modules.assignment: A medical test is 95% accurate. Given that 2% of the population has a disease, use Bayes' theorem to find the probability that a positive test is a true positive. Draw a tree diagram for the full solution.

modules.title: Discrete Probability Distributions
modules.objectives: Define a discrete probability distribution; compute expected value and variance; identify binomial settings; apply the binomial probability formula.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 4.1–4.3: Discrete Distributions & Binomial
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Binomial Distribution
modules.assignment: A fair coin is flipped 10 times. Construct the full probability distribution table for X = number of heads. Compute the expected value, variance, and the probability of getting exactly 6 heads.

modules.title: Sampling Distributions
modules.objectives: State the Central Limit Theorem; describe sampling distributions of the sample mean and sample proportion; apply CLT to calculate probabilities for sample means.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 7.1–7.3: Central Limit Theorem
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Central Limit Theorem
modules.video2Note: StatQuest — Central Limit Theorem, Clearly Explained
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Probability of Sample Mean
modules.assignment: A population has mean 50 and SD 10. For samples of n = 25 and n = 100, compute the standard error, describe the shape of the sampling distribution, and find the probability that the sample mean exceeds 52.

modules.title: Confidence Intervals: Means
modules.objectives: Explain confidence interval logic; identify when to use t vs. z; construct a one-sample t-interval; correctly interpret a confidence interval in context.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 8.1–8.2: Confidence Intervals for Means
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — One-Sample t-Interval for a Mean
modules.assignment: A sample of 20 students has a mean test score of 78 with sample SD 9. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the population mean using the t-distribution. State the critical value, margin of error, and interpret the interval in one complete sentence.

modules.title: Confidence Intervals: Proportions
modules.objectives: Construct a one-proportion z-interval; compute margin of error; determine sample size needed for a desired margin of error; interpret the interval in context.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 8.3: Confidence Interval for a Proportion
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — One-Proportion z-Interval
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #26 — Confidence Intervals for Proportions
modules.assignment: In a survey of 400 voters, 220 support a ballot measure. Construct a 99% confidence interval for the true proportion. Compute the margin of error. Then determine how large a sample is needed to achieve a margin of error of ±3% at 95% confidence.

modules.title: Hypothesis Testing: Means
modules.objectives: State null and alternative hypotheses; determine p-value and compare to significance level; identify Type I and Type II errors; conduct a one-sample t-test.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 9.1–9.4: Hypothesis Testing for Means
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — One-Sample t-Test for a Mean
modules.video2Note: StatQuest — Hypothesis Testing and p-Values, Clearly Explained
modules.assignment: A manufacturer claims its batteries last 500 hours on average. A sample of 25 batteries has a mean of 487 hours and SD of 30 hours. At α = 0.05, conduct a full one-sample t-test: state hypotheses, compute t-statistic and p-value, make a decision, and state a conclusion in context.

modules.title: Hypothesis Testing: Proportions & Chi-Square
modules.objectives: Conduct a one-proportion z-test; perform chi-square goodness-of-fit test; perform chi-square test of independence; interpret results in context.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 11.1–11.4: Chi-Square Tests
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Chi-Square Goodness of Fit
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Chi-Square Test of Independence Practice
modules.assignment: A die is rolled 120 times. Expected count for each face is 20. Observed counts are: 1→18, 2→22, 3→17, 4→25, 5→19, 6→19. Conduct a full chi-square goodness-of-fit test at α = 0.05.

modules.title: Linear Regression
modules.objectives: Interpret scatterplots; compute and interpret the correlation coefficient r; find the least-squares regression line; analyze residuals; interpret r².
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 12.1–12.5: Linear Regression & Correlation
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Least-Squares Regression
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Calculating the Regression Line
modules.assignment: Using a dataset of 10 (x, y) pairs provided by your instructor, calculate r, the regression equation (slope and intercept), and r². Draw the scatterplot, plot the regression line, and compute residuals for each point. Identify any influential points.

modules.title: Statistical Ethics & Real-World Applications
modules.objectives: Identify misleading statistics and graphs; understand data privacy principles; design an original study with proper controls; critically evaluate statistical claims in media.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 1.4: Experimental Design and Ethics
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Statistics — Experimental Design
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #41 — How to Spot a Misleading Graph
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Identifying Bias in Samples
modules.assignment: Find two statistical claims in news media this week. For each: (1) identify the statistic used, (2) evaluate whether the claim is well-supported, (3) identify any misleading framing or omitted context. Then design a simple 2-variable study on a topic of your choice, including sampling method, controls, and ethical considerations.

questions.question: A researcher surveys every 10th person on a voter registration list. This is an example of which sampling method?
questions.explanation: Systematic sampling selects every kth element from a list after a random start.

questions.question: Which of the following is a categorical variable?
questions.explanation: Categorical variables represent groups or labels, not numerical measurements.

questions.question: A histogram is most appropriate for displaying which type of data?
options: Categorical data with a few categories
questions.explanation: Histograms group continuous numerical values into bins and display their frequencies.

questions.question: For the data set {3, 5, 7, 8, 12, 15, 20}, what is the IQR?
questions.explanation: Q1 = 5, Q3 = 15 (median of lower and upper halves). IQR = 15 − 5 = 10.

questions.question: A data set has mean 70 and standard deviation 5. A score of 80 has a z-score of:
According to the empirical rule, approximately what percentage of data falls within two standard deviations of the mean in a normal distribution?
questions.explanation: The empirical rule states 68% within 1 SD, 95% within 2 SD, 99.7% within 3 SD.

questions.question: A bag contains 4 red and 6 blue marbles. If one marble is drawn at random, what is the probability it is red?
Events A and B are mutually exclusive. P(A) = 0.3 and P(B) = 0.4. What is P(A or B)?
questions.explanation: For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) = 0.3 + 0.4 = 0.7.

questions.question: P(A) = 0.5, P(B) = 0.4, P(A and B) = 0.2. Are A and B independent?
options: Yes, because P(A and B) = P(A)·P(B)
No, because P(A and B) ≠ P(A)·P(B)
Yes, because they are mutually exclusive
questions.answer: Yes, because P(A and B) = P(A)·P(B)
questions.explanation: P(A)·P(B) = 0.5 × 0.4 = 0.2 = P(A and B), so A and B are independent.

questions.question: A binomial experiment has n = 8 and p = 0.5. What is the expected number of successes?
questions.explanation: Expected value of a binomial: E(X) = np = 8 × 0.5 = 4.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a condition for a binomial distribution?
options: Probability of success changes each trial
Only two possible outcomes per trial
questions.answer: Probability of success changes each trial
questions.explanation: A binomial distribution requires a constant probability of success across all trials.

questions.question: The Central Limit Theorem states that as sample size increases, the sampling distribution of the sample mean approaches:
questions.explanation: The CLT guarantees the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal for large n, regardless of the population's shape.

questions.question: A population has standard deviation σ = 20. For samples of size n = 100, the standard error of the mean is:
questions.explanation: Standard error = σ / √n = 20 / √100 = 20 / 10 = 2.

questions.question: A spinner is divided into 5 equal sections numbered 1 through 5. Define the sample space. Then calculate P(spinning an odd number) and P(spinning a number greater than 3).
questions.explanation: Odd numbers: 1, 3, 5 → 3 out of 5 equally likely outcomes. Greater than 3: 4, 5 → 2 out of 5.

questions.question: The ages of 7 employees are: 24, 28, 31, 35, 35, 40, 52. Compute the mean and median. Then explain which measure better represents the center of this data set and why.
questions.answer: Mean = 245/7 ≈ 35.0; Median = 35; The median is a better measure because the value 52 is a potential outlier that pulls the mean slightly upward.
questions.explanation: Mean: (24+28+31+35+35+40+52)/7 = 245/7 ≈ 35.0. Median (middle of sorted list) = 35. Both coincide here, but the outlier at 52 makes the median more robust.

questions.question: In an observational study, the researcher:
options: Uses a control group and a placebo
questions.explanation: In an observational study, the researcher collects data without imposing any treatment or manipulation.

questions.question: Which display is best for showing the distribution shape of a large quantitative data set?
questions.explanation: Histograms reveal shape, center, and spread of large quantitative data sets through frequency bins.

questions.question: A distribution is right-skewed. Which statement is generally true?
questions.explanation: In a right-skewed distribution, the tail pulls the mean to the right, making it larger than the median.

questions.question: A data value is an outlier by the 1.5×IQR rule if it is below Q1 − 1.5·IQR or above:
questions.explanation: The upper fence is Q3 + 1.5 × IQR. Values above this fence are considered outliers.

questions.question: A student scores at the 84th percentile on a normally distributed exam with mean 70 and SD 10. Approximately what is the student's score?
questions.explanation: The 84th percentile is approximately 1 SD above the mean. Score = 70 + 10 = 80.

questions.question: If P(A) = 0.6 and A and B are independent with P(B) = 0.3, what is P(A and B)?
questions.explanation: For independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) = 0.6 × 0.3 = 0.18.

questions.question: P(B|A) = 0.4, P(A) = 0.5. What is P(A and B)?
questions.explanation: Multiplication rule: P(A and B) = P(B|A) × P(A) = 0.4 × 0.5 = 0.20.

questions.question: A discrete random variable X has E(X) = 3 and E(X²) = 13. What is Var(X)?
As sample size increases, the standard error of the mean:
questions.explanation: Standard error = σ/√n. As n grows, √n grows, so the standard error decreases.

questions.question: A 95% confidence interval for a population mean is (48, 56). Which interpretation is correct?
options: The population mean is 52 with probability 0.95
95% of all data values lie between 48 and 56
We are 95% confident the true population mean lies between 48 and 56
5% of sample means fall outside this interval
questions.answer: We are 95% confident the true population mean lies between 48 and 56
questions.explanation: A confidence interval means we used a method that captures the true parameter in 95% of repeated samples.

questions.question: Which of the following increases the margin of error of a confidence interval for a mean?
questions.explanation: Margin of error = z* × (σ/√n). A larger σ directly increases the margin of error.

questions.question: In a hypothesis test, the p-value is 0.03 and α = 0.05. The correct decision is to:
questions.explanation: Since p = 0.03 < α = 0.05, the result is statistically significant and we reject the null hypothesis.

questions.question: A Type II error occurs when the test:
options: Fails to reject a false null hypothesis
Fails to reject a true null hypothesis
questions.answer: Fails to reject a false null hypothesis
questions.explanation: Type II error (β): failing to detect a real effect when one truly exists.

questions.question: A chi-square goodness-of-fit test is used to determine whether:
options: Observed frequencies match expected frequencies for one variable
A population proportion equals a claimed value
questions.answer: Observed frequencies match expected frequencies for one variable
questions.explanation: The goodness-of-fit test compares observed counts in categories to theoretically expected counts.

questions.question: If the correlation coefficient r = −0.85, which statement best describes the relationship?
questions.explanation: r close to −1 indicates a strong negative linear association between the two variables.

questions.question: For the regression line ŷ = 3.2x + 10, what does the slope 3.2 represent?
options: The predicted increase in y for each one-unit increase in x
The residual for the mean x-value
questions.answer: The predicted increase in y for each one-unit increase in x
questions.explanation: The slope is the rate of change: for each 1-unit increase in x, y is predicted to increase by 3.2.

questions.question: If r² = 0.64 for a regression model, which statement is correct?
options: 64% of the variation in x is explained by y
64% of the variation in y is explained by x
questions.answer: 64% of the variation in y is explained by x
questions.explanation: r² (coefficient of determination) is the proportion of variability in y accounted for by the linear relationship with x.

questions.question: A pollster samples 1,000 voters and finds 58% support a policy. The margin of error at 95% confidence is ±3%. Which statement is valid?
options: 58% of all voters support the policy
We are certain the true proportion is between 55% and 61%
We are 95% confident the true proportion is between 55% and 61%
The sample size is too small to draw any conclusion
questions.answer: We are 95% confident the true proportion is between 55% and 61%
questions.explanation: The confidence interval is 58% ± 3% = (55%, 61%). We interpret it as 95% confidence, not certainty.

questions.question: A graph that starts its y-axis at 50 instead of 0 to exaggerate differences between bars is an example of:
questions.explanation: Truncating the y-axis makes small differences appear much larger, misleading the viewer.

questions.question: A study finds a strong positive correlation (r = 0.91) between ice cream sales and drowning rates across months. A headline reads 'Ice cream causes drowning.' Explain what is wrong with this conclusion and identify what statistical concept is being ignored.
questions.answer: Correlation does not imply causation; a lurking variable (hot weather) explains both increases.
questions.explanation: Both ice cream sales and drowning rates increase in summer due to hot weather — a confounding/lurking variable. Correlation measures association, not causation.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a quantitative variable?
quizQuestions.explanation: Height is a numerical measurement that can be compared and computed — a quantitative variable.

quizQuestions.question: An experiment differs from an observational study because it:
quizQuestions.explanation: In an experiment, the researcher actively imposes a treatment and measures the response.

quizQuestions.question: A sample where every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected is called a:
quizQuestions.explanation: Simple random sampling gives every individual and every group of that size an equal chance of selection.

quizQuestions.question: Which is an example of response bias?
options: Respondents lying about income on a survey
Only people who feel strongly respond
quizQuestions.answer: Respondents lying about income on a survey
quizQuestions.explanation: Response bias occurs when participants give inaccurate answers, often due to social desirability or question wording.

quizQuestions.question: In a stratified sample, the population is first divided into:
quizQuestions.explanation: Stratified sampling divides the population into strata (e.g., by grade level) and then randomly samples within each stratum.

quizQuestions.question: A box plot displays which five summary statistics?
quizQuestions.explanation: The five-number summary shown in a box plot: minimum, first quartile, median, third quartile, maximum.

quizQuestions.question: A histogram with a long tail to the right is described as:
quizQuestions.explanation: A right (positive) skew means the longer tail extends toward higher values on the right.

quizQuestions.question: Which graph is best for comparing the distribution of a numerical variable across two groups?
quizQuestions.explanation: Side-by-side box plots allow visual comparison of center, spread, and skew across groups.

quizQuestions.question: In a stem-and-leaf plot for the value 37, the stem is:
quizQuestions.explanation: In a stem-and-leaf plot, the tens digit (3) is the stem and the units digit (7) is the leaf.

quizQuestions.question: A bar graph differs from a histogram in that:
options: Bar graphs show frequency; histograms do not
Histograms have gaps between bars; bar graphs do not
Bar graphs are for categorical data; histograms are for quantitative data
Histograms can only show five values
quizQuestions.answer: Bar graphs are for categorical data; histograms are for quantitative data
quizQuestions.explanation: Bar graphs display categorical variables with separated bars; histograms display continuous numerical data with adjacent bars.

quizQuestions.question: Which measure of center is most affected by outliers?
quizQuestions.explanation: The mean uses all data values in its calculation, so extreme values (outliers) pull it strongly.

quizQuestions.question: Find the median of: 4, 7, 9, 12, 15.
quizQuestions.explanation: Sorted list: 4, 7, 9, 12, 15. The middle value (3rd of 5) is 9.

quizQuestions.question: The IQR is defined as:
quizQuestions.explanation: IQR (Interquartile Range) = Q3 − Q1, representing the spread of the middle 50% of data.

quizQuestions.question: A data value is an outlier if it is more than 1.5 × IQR above Q3. If Q3 = 50 and IQR = 12, what is the upper fence?
Standard deviation measures:
options: The average distance of data values from the mean
The difference between max and min
quizQuestions.answer: The average distance of data values from the mean
quizQuestions.explanation: Standard deviation quantifies how spread out values are around the mean.

quizQuestions.question: What shape does a normal distribution have?
quizQuestions.explanation: The normal distribution is perfectly symmetric around its mean and bell-shaped.

quizQuestions.question: A test score of 85 has a z-score of 1.0. The mean is 75. What is the standard deviation?
In a normal distribution with mean 100 and SD 15, what percentage of values lie between 85 and 115?
quizQuestions.explanation: 85 and 115 are each 1 SD from the mean (100 ± 15). The empirical rule states 68% of data lies within 1 SD.

quizQuestions.question: A z-score of −2 means the value is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Negative z-scores indicate the value is below the mean. z = −2 means 2 SDs below.

quizQuestions.question: Using the standard normal table, P(Z < 1.96) ≈:
quizQuestions.explanation: The standard normal table gives P(Z < 1.96) ≈ 0.975, which is why z* = 1.96 is used for 95% confidence intervals.

quizQuestions.question: When flipping a fair coin, what is the probability of getting heads?
quizQuestions.explanation: A fair coin has two equally likely outcomes: heads and tails. P(heads) = 1/2 = 0.5.

quizQuestions.question: Two events are mutually exclusive if:
options: They cannot both occur at the same time
quizQuestions.answer: They cannot both occur at the same time
quizQuestions.explanation: Mutually exclusive events have no outcomes in common; P(A and B) = 0.

quizQuestions.question: P(A) = 0.7. What is P(not A)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Complement rule: P(not A) = 1 − P(A) = 1 − 0.7 = 0.3.

quizQuestions.question: A single card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is P(drawing a king or a heart)?
quizQuestions.explanation: P(king) = 4/52, P(heart) = 13/52, P(king and heart) = 1/52. P(king or heart) = 4/52 + 13/52 − 1/52 = 16/52.

quizQuestions.question: The sum of all probabilities in a valid probability distribution must equal:
quizQuestions.explanation: A valid probability distribution requires that all probabilities are non-negative and sum to exactly 1.

quizQuestions.question: P(A|B) is read as:
options: The probability of A and B
The probability of A given B
The probability of B given A
The probability of A or B
quizQuestions.answer: The probability of A given B
quizQuestions.explanation: Conditional probability P(A|B) means the probability of event A occurring, given that B has already occurred.

quizQuestions.question: If P(A and B) = 0.12, P(B) = 0.4, what is P(A|B)?
Two events are independent if:
quizQuestions.explanation: Independence means knowing B occurred does not change the probability of A: P(A|B) = P(A).

quizQuestions.question: A tree diagram is used to:
options: Visualize sequential probability events and their outcomes
quizQuestions.answer: Visualize sequential probability events and their outcomes
quizQuestions.explanation: Tree diagrams branch out to show all possible outcomes of sequential events and their associated probabilities.

quizQuestions.question: In a contingency table, if P(B|A) = P(B), the events A and B are:
quizQuestions.explanation: Independence is defined by P(B|A) = P(B) — knowing A gives no information about B.

quizQuestions.question: For a discrete probability distribution, all probabilities must be:
options: Between 0 and 1 inclusive, and sum to 1
quizQuestions.answer: Between 0 and 1 inclusive, and sum to 1
quizQuestions.explanation: Valid probabilities are non-negative, at most 1, and all possible outcome probabilities sum to 1.

quizQuestions.question: Which scenario is a binomial setting?
options: Drawing cards without replacement from a deck
Rolling a die until a 6 appears
Flipping a coin 20 times and counting heads
quizQuestions.answer: Flipping a coin 20 times and counting heads
quizQuestions.explanation: Fixed n = 20, two outcomes (H/T), constant probability, independent trials — all binomial conditions are met.

quizQuestions.question: The formula for binomial probability P(X = k) is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The binomial formula multiplies the combination C(n,k) by the probability of exactly k successes and (n−k) failures.

quizQuestions.question: For a binomial distribution with n = 10 and p = 0.3, the variance is:
Expected value of a random variable tells you:
options: The average outcome over many repetitions
quizQuestions.answer: The average outcome over many repetitions
quizQuestions.explanation: E(X) is the long-run average value of the random variable over infinitely many trials.

quizQuestions.question: The Central Limit Theorem applies when:
quizQuestions.explanation: The CLT guarantees an approximately normal sampling distribution when n ≥ 30, regardless of population shape.

quizQuestions.question: The standard error of the sample mean equals:
quizQuestions.explanation: Standard error of the mean = σ/√n, measuring how much the sample mean varies from sample to sample.

quizQuestions.question: A population has μ = 40 and σ = 8. For n = 16, what is the mean of the sampling distribution of x̄?
quizQuestions.explanation: The mean of the sampling distribution of x̄ always equals the population mean μ = 40.

quizQuestions.question: Increasing sample size from n = 25 to n = 100 reduces the standard error by a factor of:
quizQuestions.explanation: SE = σ/√n. √100/√25 = 10/5 = 2. So the SE is halved — reduced by a factor of 2.

quizQuestions.question: The sampling distribution of p̂ (sample proportion) is approximately normal when:
quizQuestions.explanation: The normal approximation for p̂ is valid when both np and n(1−p) are at least 10.

quizQuestions.question: When should you use a t-distribution instead of z for a confidence interval for a mean?
options: When population standard deviation is unknown
quizQuestions.answer: When population standard deviation is unknown
quizQuestions.explanation: The t-distribution is used when σ is unknown and s (sample SD) is used instead.

quizQuestions.question: Degrees of freedom for a one-sample t-interval with n = 20 is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Degrees of freedom for a one-sample t-procedure: df = n − 1 = 20 − 1 = 19.

quizQuestions.question: A 99% confidence interval is _________ than a 95% confidence interval from the same data.
quizQuestions.explanation: Higher confidence requires a larger critical value, which increases the margin of error and widens the interval.

quizQuestions.question: Margin of error in a confidence interval is defined as:
quizQuestions.explanation: Margin of error = t* × (s/√n) or z* × (σ/√n), combining the critical value and standard error.

quizQuestions.question: Which statement correctly interprets a 95% CI of (62, 70) for a mean?
options: We are 95% confident the population mean is between 62 and 70
There is a 95% chance the true mean is exactly 66
quizQuestions.answer: We are 95% confident the population mean is between 62 and 70
quizQuestions.explanation: Confidence intervals are statements about the parameter (population mean), not individuals or the sample mean.

quizQuestions.question: The formula for the margin of error of a proportion confidence interval is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Margin of error for a proportion: ME = z* × √(p̂(1−p̂)/n).

quizQuestions.question: In a sample of 200, 80 said yes. What is the sample proportion p̂?
To reduce the margin of error by half, you must multiply the sample size by:
quizQuestions.explanation: ME ∝ 1/√n. To halve ME, you need √n to double, so n must be multiplied by 4.

quizQuestions.question: When no prior estimate of p is available, which value is used to conservatively calculate required sample size?
quizQuestions.explanation: p = 0.5 maximizes p(1−p), giving the largest (most conservative) required sample size.

quizQuestions.question: A 95% confidence interval for a proportion is (0.42, 0.58). The point estimate is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The point estimate is the midpoint of the interval: (0.42 + 0.58)/2 = 0.50.

quizQuestions.question: The null hypothesis (H₀) typically states:
options: There is no effect or no difference
quizQuestions.answer: There is no effect or no difference
quizQuestions.explanation: The null hypothesis is the default assumption of no effect, no difference, or a specific claimed value.

quizQuestions.question: A p-value of 0.001 at α = 0.05 means:
options: Reject H₀; strong evidence against it
quizQuestions.answer: Reject H₀; strong evidence against it
quizQuestions.explanation: p = 0.001 < 0.05 = α. We reject H₀; the extremely small p-value suggests strong evidence against the null.

quizQuestions.question: A Type I error is:
options: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis
quizQuestions.explanation: Type I error (α): incorrectly rejecting H₀ when it is actually true — a false positive.

quizQuestions.question: The t-test statistic for a one-sample test is computed as:
quizQuestions.explanation: The one-sample t-statistic uses the sample SD (s) since population σ is unknown.

quizQuestions.question: A two-tailed test with α = 0.05 splits the rejection region into:
quizQuestions.explanation: For a two-tailed test, the significance level α is split equally: α/2 = 0.025 in each tail.

quizQuestions.question: The chi-square statistic is calculated as:
quizQuestions.explanation: Chi-square: χ² = Σ(Observed − Expected)² / Expected, summed over all categories.

quizQuestions.question: Degrees of freedom for a chi-square goodness-of-fit test with 5 categories is:
quizQuestions.explanation: df = number of categories − 1 = 5 − 1 = 4.

quizQuestions.question: A chi-square test of independence tests whether:
options: Two categorical variables are associated in a population
Observed proportions match a claimed value
A single mean equals a null value
quizQuestions.answer: Two categorical variables are associated in a population
quizQuestions.explanation: The chi-square test of independence examines whether two categorical variables are related or independent.

quizQuestions.question: For a one-proportion z-test with p₀ = 0.5, n = 100, and p̂ = 0.58, the z-statistic is:
A large chi-square statistic means:
options: Observed and expected frequencies are very close
There is strong evidence against H₀
The sample size is too small
quizQuestions.answer: There is strong evidence against H₀
quizQuestions.explanation: A large χ² value indicates large discrepancies between observed and expected counts, giving evidence against H₀.

quizQuestions.question: Which value of r indicates the strongest linear relationship?
quizQuestions.explanation: The strength of correlation is measured by |r|. |−0.85| = 0.85 is the largest among the options.

quizQuestions.question: In the regression equation ŷ = 2x + 5, what is the predicted y when x = 3?
A residual is defined as:
quizQuestions.explanation: Residual = actual observed value minus the predicted value from the regression line.

quizQuestions.question: If r = 0.9, what is r²?
quizQuestions.explanation: r² = (0.9)² = 0.81. This means 81% of the variability in y is explained by x.

quizQuestions.question: A scatterplot shows a positive linear trend. This means:
options: As x increases, y tends to decrease
As x increases, y tends to increase
x and y are not related
quizQuestions.answer: As x increases, y tends to increase
quizQuestions.explanation: A positive association means higher x values correspond to higher y values on average.

quizQuestions.question: A graph that uses 3D effects to exaggerate differences in a pie chart is an example of:
quizQuestions.explanation: 3D effects distort the relative sizes of pie slices, making some appear larger than they actually are.

quizQuestions.question: Correlation between ice cream sales and drowning rates is high. This means:
options: A lurking variable (e.g., hot weather) may explain both
quizQuestions.answer: A lurking variable (e.g., hot weather) may explain both
quizQuestions.explanation: Correlation does not imply causation. A third variable (hot summer weather) drives both variables.

quizQuestions.question: Data privacy in statistics means researchers must:
options: Protect the identities and sensitive information of participants
Share raw data with all readers
quizQuestions.answer: Protect the identities and sensitive information of participants
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethical research protects participant confidentiality, often through anonymization and informed consent.

quizQuestions.question: A study reports 'the drug reduced symptoms by 50%.' To evaluate this claim, you should ask:
options: What is the sample size and control group?
What country was the study in?
quizQuestions.answer: What is the sample size and control group?
quizQuestions.explanation: Evaluating statistical claims requires knowing the study design: sample size, control group, randomization, and confounders.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of voluntary response bias?
options: Selecting every 5th person on a list
Only people who feel strongly about a topic respond to an online poll
Dividing by gender and sampling each group proportionally
Using a random number generator to select participants
quizQuestions.answer: Only people who feel strongly about a topic respond to an online poll
quizQuestions.explanation: Voluntary response samples attract people with strong opinions, making them unrepresentative of the general population.
